concepts.title: Use dark pen, not faint pencil

questions.text: Faint pencil is fine for Section B as long as the working is correct.
questions.explanation: In 2024, many students responded in faint pencil; papers are scanned for marking.

questions.text: Use dark pen or pencil; faint work may not scan clearly and steps could be missed.
questions.explanation: Assessors mark scanned images, so visibility matters.
questions.studyTip: Make every line dark enough to scan.

questions.text: You can skip intermediate steps in a calculation if you reach the right final answer.
questions.explanation: In 2024, omitted steps cost marks.

questions.text: Show each step of your working; if steps are omitted, full marks may not be awarded.
questions.explanation: Method marks reward the reasoning, not just the answer.

questions.text: For a 'show that' question, writing the given target answer is enough.
questions.explanation: In 2023, 'show that' marks were for the working, and students had to check it was correct.

questions.text: 'Show that' marks are for the working; demonstrate each step leading to the stated result.
questions.explanation: You cannot earn the mark by simply restating the provided answer.
questions.studyTip: Derive the result line by line.

concepts.title: Plan a strategy for multi-step problems

questions.text: The best approach to a hard multi-step problem is to start writing formulas and hope a path appears.
questions.explanation: Across years, many students had no strategy for solving multi-step problems.

questions.text: Multi-step problems need a planned strategy that links the physics steps before calculating.
questions.explanation: Identify what each step gives you and how it feeds the next.
questions.studyTip: Map the chain of steps first.

questions.text: If your calculation gives a path difference of 1020 m, you should just report it.
questions.explanation: In 2023, students reported an absurd '1020 m' path difference without recognising the error.

questions.text: Check whether an answer is physically realistic; an absurd value signals an error, often a unit slip.
questions.explanation: A path difference of metres for light is clearly wrong.

concepts.title: Use the correct formula for the situation

questions.text: Classical formulas like half m v squared can be used for particles travelling near the speed of light.
questions.explanation: In 2023 and 2024, using classical rather than relativistic physics was a common error.

questions.text: At relativistic speeds you must use relativistic formulas such as Ek = (gamma - 1) m c squared.
questions.explanation: Classical kinetic energy underestimates energy at high speeds.
questions.studyTip: Check the speed before choosing the formula.

questions.text: Quantities like nm, km or GV can be used directly in formulas without conversion.
questions.explanation: Across 2022 to 2025, unit-conversion and power-of-ten mistakes were the most common errors.

questions.text: Convert all quantities to SI units (nm to m, km to m, GV to V) before substituting into formulas.
questions.explanation: Mixed units produce errors of many powers of ten.

questions.text: The gradient of a line is calculated as run divided by rise.
questions.explanation: In 2022, calculating run over rise was a common error.

questions.text: Gradient is rise divided by run (change in y over change in x).
questions.explanation: Choose two well-separated points to reduce reading error.
questions.studyTip: Gradient = change in y / change in x.

concepts.title: Use points on the line of best fit

questions.text: To find a graph's gradient, use any two data points from the table.
questions.explanation: In 2023 and 2025, students used data points that were not on the line of best fit.

questions.text: Use two points that lie on your line of best fit, not raw data points off the line.
questions.explanation: The line of best fit, not individual data, gives the trend.

concepts.title: Line of best fit follows the trend

questions.text: A line of best fit should pass through the first and last data points.
questions.explanation: In 2024, putting the line through the first and last points was the most common error.

questions.text: A line of best fit follows the overall trend with points balanced either side; it need not hit the end points.
questions.explanation: Roughly equal numbers of points sit above and below the line.
questions.studyTip: Don't anchor the line to the end points.

questions.text: If a work-function intercept sits at 3 on the energy axis, you should read the work function as -3 eV.
questions.explanation: In 2023, students read the work function as -3 eV rather than 3 eV.

questions.text: Read the magnitude correctly: a work function is a positive energy, such as 3 eV.
questions.explanation: Mind the axis direction and sign when reading values.
questions.studyTip: A work function is a positive energy.

questions.text: Reading a value off a graph is the same as interpreting what it means for the physics.
questions.explanation: In 2023, students could read graphs but struggled to interpret or apply the reading.

questions.text: After reading a value you must interpret and apply it to answer the question.
questions.explanation: Marks come from using the value, not just stating it.
questions.studyTip: Read it, then explain its meaning.

concepts.title: Area under a force-time graph is impulse

questions.text: The area under a force-time graph gives the kinetic energy.
questions.explanation: In 2025, students equated the area to the wrong quantity.

questions.text: The area under a force-time graph is the impulse, equal to the change in momentum.
questions.explanation: Impulse = F times t = change in momentum.
questions.studyTip: Area under F-t = impulse = change in p.

concepts.title: Projectile range uses twice the angle

questions.text: In the range equation R = u squared sin(2 theta) / g, you substitute the launch angle theta for 2 theta.
questions.explanation: In 2022, using 30 degrees instead of 60 degrees (for 2 theta) was the most common error.
questions.studyTip: Double the angle in the sine.

questions.text: The range equation uses sin(2 theta); for a 30 degree launch you use sin(60 degrees).
questions.explanation: The factor of two inside the sine is easy to miss.

concepts.title: Air resistance reduces height and range

questions.text: Air resistance on a projectile reduces the horizontal range but not the maximum height.
questions.explanation: In 2024, most identified reduced distance but failed to mention reduced height.

questions.text: Air resistance reduces both the range and the maximum height of a projectile.
questions.explanation: Energy is removed throughout the flight, lowering the peak and shortening the path.
questions.studyTip: Lower and shorter with air resistance.

questions.text: You can take acceleration due to gravity as positive (upwards) when analysing a projectile.
questions.explanation: In 2024, students had g acting upwards, breaking their sign convention.

questions.text: Choose one direction as positive and keep it; gravity acts downward, so g is negative if up is positive.
questions.explanation: Inconsistent signs lead to wrong directions and magnitudes.
questions.studyTip: Pick positive, then g points the other way (down).

questions.text: The centripetal force on an object in circular motion is F = m v / r.
questions.explanation: In 2022, forgetting to square the velocity (or using a different formula) was common.

questions.text: Centripetal force is F = m v squared / r, with the velocity squared.
questions.explanation: Doubling the speed quadruples the required force.

concepts.title: Normal force on a banked track

questions.text: On a banked track the normal force points horizontally inward toward the centre of the turn.
questions.explanation: In 2024, students said the incline made the normal force point inward to the centre.
questions.studyTip: Normal is perpendicular to the surface.

questions.text: The normal force is perpendicular to the track surface; its horizontal component provides the centripetal force.
questions.explanation: Resolve the normal force to find the centre-pointing component.
questions.studyTip: Horizontal part of the normal force = centripetal.

concepts.title: Friction provides centripetal force on a flat road

questions.text: When a car turns on a flat road, the friction at the tyres acts backward and is unrelated to circular motion.
questions.explanation: In 2022, students struggled to link tyre friction to a centripetal force and its direction.

questions.text: On a flat road, friction at the tyres points toward the centre of the turn, providing the centripetal force.
questions.explanation: Without that friction the car cannot turn.
questions.studyTip: Turning on the flat: friction is centripetal.

questions.text: The r found from r = cube root of (G M T squared / 4 pi squared) is the satellite's altitude above the surface.
questions.explanation: In 2022 and 2025, forgetting to subtract Earth's radius for altitude was the most common error.

questions.text: That r is measured from Earth's centre; subtract Earth's radius to get the altitude.
questions.explanation: Orbital equations give the distance from the centre, not the surface.

questions.text: The gravitational field strength g stays constant as a spacecraft moves far from Earth.
questions.explanation: In 2025, a student wrongly assumed g remains constant as the spacecraft moved away.

questions.text: g decreases with distance from Earth (g is proportional to 1 over r squared), so it is not constant with altitude.
questions.explanation: Treating g as constant only works near the surface.
questions.studyTip: g is proportional to 1 / r squared.

questions.text: Identifying that momentum is conserved is enough; you do not need to write it mathematically.
questions.explanation: In 2022, students named the principle but could not express it in mathematical form.

questions.text: You must express conservation of momentum mathematically: total momentum before equals total momentum after.
questions.explanation: Set up the equation with masses and velocities to earn the marks.
questions.studyTip: Sum of m v before = sum of m v after.

questions.text: A collision is inelastic because momentum is not conserved.
questions.explanation: In 2024, students wrongly said momentum is not conserved, or did not specify kinetic energy.

questions.text: Momentum is conserved in all collisions; a collision is inelastic when kinetic energy is not conserved.
questions.explanation: Use kinetic energy, not momentum, to classify the collision.
questions.studyTip: Elastic vs inelastic is about kinetic energy.

concepts.title: Only changing flux induces an EMF

questions.text: A steady, constant magnetic flux through a coil induces an EMF.
questions.explanation: In 2023 and 2025, vague statements about flux were penalised; it is the change in flux that matters.

questions.text: Only a changing magnetic flux induces an EMF (emf = -N times change in flux over change in time).
questions.explanation: A constant flux produces no induced voltage.
questions.studyTip: Induced EMF needs the flux to change.

questions.text: For an induced-EMF problem you can use the full period as the time interval regardless of the fraction of a cycle.
questions.explanation: In 2022, incorrectly calculating the time interval was the most common error.
questions.studyTip: Match the time to the cycle fraction.

questions.text: Use the time for the relevant part of the cycle (for example a quarter period) as the time interval.
questions.explanation: The rate of change of flux depends on the correct interval.
questions.studyTip: Use the time for the portion described.

concepts.title: Apply Lenz's law, don't just define it

questions.text: Quoting the definition of Lenz's law answers a question about the direction of an induced current.
questions.explanation: In 2025, many gave a definition of Lenz's law rather than applying it.

questions.text: You must apply Lenz's law: state how the flux changes, then give the induced current that opposes that change.
questions.explanation: The induced current opposes the change in flux that produced it.
questions.studyTip: Flux change first, then the opposing current.

concepts.title: Changing flux, not a field that flips

questions.text: An EMF is induced in a rotating loop because the magnetic field changes direction.
questions.explanation: In 2025, many stated the field 'changed direction' rather than describing changing flux.
questions.studyTip: It's the flux through the loop.

questions.text: An EMF is induced because the flux through the loop increases and decreases as it rotates, not because the field reverses.
questions.explanation: The external field is steady; the flux through the loop varies with orientation.
questions.studyTip: Flux through the loop changes as it turns.

questions.text: In an ideal transformer the primary and secondary have the same voltage and the same current.
questions.explanation: In 2023, students confused the transformer ratios and miscalculated currents.

questions.text: In an ideal transformer Vp / Vs = Np / Ns = Is / Ip, so stepping voltage up steps current down.
questions.explanation: Power in equals power out, so higher voltage means lower current.
questions.studyTip: Step up voltage, step down current.

questions.text: To use AC values in a power calculation you should convert RMS values to peak by multiplying by root two.
questions.explanation: In 2023 and 2024, wrongly converting RMS to peak was a common error.

questions.text: Power and most calculations use RMS values; only convert to peak (RMS times root two) when the question asks for peak.
questions.explanation: Quoted mains values are RMS unless stated otherwise.
questions.studyTip: Peak = RMS times root two, only when needed.

questions.text: Power lost in transmission lines is found from the supply voltage at the start of the lines.
questions.explanation: In 2025, students did not use line current and the voltage drop to find power loss.
questions.studyTip: Use line current and line resistance.

questions.text: Power loss in the lines is I squared R; find the line current first, using the voltage drop across the lines.
questions.explanation: It is the current in the lines and their resistance that determine the loss.
questions.studyTip: P loss = I squared R for the lines.

concepts.title: Why step down at the destination

questions.text: Electricity is transmitted at high voltage and used at high voltage, so no step-down is needed.
questions.explanation: In 2024 and 2025, many explained stepping up but not why stepping down at the destination is required.
questions.studyTip: Up for transmission, down for use.

questions.text: Voltage is stepped up for transmission (to cut I squared R loss) then stepped down for safe use at the destination.
questions.explanation: High voltage is efficient to transmit but unsafe to use directly.
questions.studyTip: Step up to send, step down to use safely.

questions.text: A DC motor uses slip rings to keep the current flowing in one direction.
questions.explanation: In 2024 and 2025, students misnamed the commutator and were unclear on its function.

questions.text: A DC motor uses a split-ring commutator that reverses the current each half-turn; slip rings are used for AC.
questions.explanation: The commutator keeps the torque in one rotational direction.

concepts.title: No force when current is parallel to the field

questions.text: A current-carrying wire always experiences a force when placed in a magnetic field.
questions.explanation: In 2025, most did not explain there is no force when the current is parallel to the field.

questions.text: There is no force on a current-carrying wire when the current is parallel to the field (the angle is zero).
questions.explanation: Force F = n B I L sin(theta) is zero when theta is zero.
questions.studyTip: Maximum force when perpendicular; zero when parallel.

questions.text: Young's double-slit pattern is primarily a demonstration of diffraction.
questions.explanation: In 2022 and 2025, students wrongly attributed the finding to diffraction.

questions.text: Young's double-slit experiment demonstrates interference; diffraction only creates the overlapping coherent beams.
questions.explanation: The bright and dark fringes are evidence of interference (and the wave model).

questions.text: In double-slit interference the fringe spacing increases when the slit separation increases.
questions.explanation: In 2025, students related fringe spacing to the wrong variable or used slit separation as the spacing.

questions.text: Fringe spacing w = lambda L / d, so it grows with wavelength and shrinks as the slit separation d increases.
questions.explanation: Wider slit separation gives more closely spaced fringes.

questions.text: A path difference of half a wavelength produces a bright fringe (constructive interference).
questions.explanation: Path-difference conditions distinguish bright from dark fringes.

questions.text: Constructive (bright) interference needs whole-number multiples of the wavelength; half-wavelength differences give dark fringes.
questions.explanation: n lambda gives bright; (n + a half) lambda gives dark.

questions.text: A path difference calculated as 1020 in micrometres can be reported in metres as 1020 m.
questions.explanation: In 2023, failing to convert micrometres to metres gave an absurd 1020 m.

questions.text: Convert micrometres to metres first; 1020 micrometres is about 1.02 times 10 to the -3 m, not 1020 m.
questions.explanation: Light path differences are tiny; metre-scale values are a red flag.

questions.text: Because 41 degrees is less than 45 degrees, total internal reflection will not occur.
questions.explanation: In 2023, students wrongly compared the angle to 45 degrees instead of the critical angle.

questions.text: Total internal reflection occurs when the angle of incidence exceeds the critical angle, not 45 degrees.
questions.explanation: Calculate the critical angle from the refractive indices first.
questions.studyTip: TIR when incidence is greater than the critical angle.

questions.text: A string fixed at both ends can support a standing wave of any wavelength.
questions.explanation: In 2023, over a third of students showed no understanding of standing waves on strings.

questions.text: A string fixed at both ends supports only standing waves whose wavelengths fit the harmonics (length = n times half a wavelength).
questions.explanation: Nodes must sit at both fixed ends.

questions.text: In Snell's law the angles of incidence and refraction are measured from the surface.
questions.explanation: In 2022, students used wrong angles by not measuring from the normal.

questions.text: Angles in Snell's law are measured from the normal, the line perpendicular to the surface.
questions.explanation: Measuring from the surface gives the complement of the correct angle.
questions.studyTip: Angles are from the normal, not the surface.

concepts.title: Diffraction depends on aperture and wavelength

questions.text: Diffraction is most pronounced when the gap is much larger than the wavelength.
questions.explanation: Significant diffraction requires the aperture to be comparable to the wavelength.
questions.studyTip: Effect is biggest when slit is near lambda.

questions.text: Diffraction is most significant when the aperture is comparable to or smaller than the wavelength.
questions.explanation: A gap much larger than the wavelength causes little spreading.
questions.studyTip: Slit close to lambda gives strong diffraction.

questions.text: In a photoelectric graph of kinetic energy against frequency, Planck's constant is the y-intercept.
questions.explanation: In 2024 and 2025, a third of students did not show that h is found from the gradient.

questions.text: The gradient of the kinetic energy versus frequency line gives Planck's constant; the intercept relates to the work function.
questions.explanation: Ek max = h f minus the work function, a straight line of gradient h.
questions.studyTip: Gradient = h; intercept relates to the work function.

questions.text: Increasing the light intensity increases the energy of each emitted photoelectron.
questions.explanation: In 2024, students confused the photon and the photoelectron.

questions.text: Intensity increases the number of photoelectrons; their maximum energy depends on frequency, not intensity.
questions.explanation: One photon ejects one electron; brighter light means more photons, not more energetic ones.
questions.studyTip: Frequency sets energy; intensity sets number.

concepts.title: Higher frequency shifts intercepts, not gradient

questions.text: Using a higher-frequency light source changes the gradient of the photoelectric kinetic-energy line.
questions.explanation: In 2024, students missed that the gradient stays the same while intercepts shift.

questions.text: Changing the metal or frequency shifts the intercepts, but the gradient (Planck's constant) stays the same.
questions.explanation: Planck's constant is universal, so the slope is unchanged.
questions.studyTip: Intercepts move; the slope (h) does not.

concepts.title: Photoelectric effect supports the particle model

questions.text: The photoelectric effect is explained equally well by the wave model of light.
questions.explanation: In 2024, students could state the particle finding but not the wave-model prediction it contradicts.

questions.text: The photoelectric effect supports the particle (photon) model; the wave model wrongly predicts energy depends on intensity.
questions.explanation: Instant emission and a threshold frequency favour photons over waves.

questions.text: The de Broglie wavelength of a particle is lambda = h f.
questions.explanation: In 2023, students used the wrong relationship or constant for matter waves.

questions.text: A particle's de Broglie wavelength is lambda = h / (m v), that is h divided by momentum.
questions.explanation: Matter waves link a particle's momentum to a wavelength.
questions.studyTip: lambda = h / p, with p = m v.

questions.text: The Lorentz factor is gamma = square root of (1 minus v squared over c squared).
questions.explanation: In 2023 and 2025, students mis-transposed the Lorentz formula or found gamma less than 1.

questions.text: The Lorentz factor is gamma = 1 / square root of (1 minus v squared over c squared), which is greater than or equal to 1.
questions.explanation: As speed approaches c, gamma grows large.
questions.studyTip: gamma = 1 / sqrt(1 - v squared / c squared).

questions.text: The kinetic energy of a particle travelling near light speed is half m v squared.
questions.explanation: In 2023, using classical half m v squared instead of relativistic kinetic energy was the most common error.

questions.text: At relativistic speeds, kinetic energy is (gamma minus 1) times m c squared.
questions.explanation: Classical kinetic energy badly underestimates energy near light speed.

concepts.title: Use the correct total mass in E = m c squared

questions.text: When two electrons are produced, use the mass of a single electron in E = m c squared.
questions.explanation: In 2024, using one electron's mass rather than the total was the most common error.

questions.text: Use the total mass converted to energy; for two electrons, use twice the electron mass.
questions.explanation: Energy released depends on the total mass involved.
questions.studyTip: E = m c squared with the total mass.

questions.text: The Michelson-Morley experiment detected a difference in the speed of light along its two perpendicular paths.
questions.explanation: In 2025, students did not identify the null result between perpendicular paths.

questions.text: The Michelson-Morley experiment found no difference in the speed of light between its two perpendicular paths.
questions.explanation: This null result undermined the idea of a light-carrying aether.
questions.studyTip: Same light speed in all directions.
